criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand the R-CDE baseline model: the Euler-type recursion for the hidden state and the continuous-time CDE formulation (Eq. 8), including the role of random Gaussian matrices A_i and the 1/sqrt(N) scaling.

The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the RF-CDE model: the random Fourier feature lift of the input path, the RF-CDE differential equation (Eq. 9), and the Euler discretization with scaling parameters sigma_A, sigma_b, and sigma_0 (Eq. 10).

The agent has read Section 3.3 to understand the R-RDE model: the Gamma_A algebra homomorphism (Eq. 11), the linear development S_t^A (Eq. 12), the Random RDE formulation (Eq. 15), and the log-ODE discretization using log-signatures and Lie brackets (Eq. 16).

The agent has read Section 4 and Appendix C.1 to understand the full experimental setup: UEA archive, preprocessing (min-max scaling to [-1,1], piecewise-linear interpolation, resampling to length 200, time and basepoint augmentation, optional lead-lag transform), SVM/LinearSVM readout, 3 runs per dataset with median accuracy, and single NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU.

The plan outlines implementation of the Random Fourier Feature map phi_mu^F (Eq. 4) that maps R^d to R^{2F} using sampled frequencies omega_j ~ Gaussian, producing (cos(omega_j^T x), sin(omega_j^T x)) components scaled by 1/sqrt(F).

The plan outlines implementation of the R-CDE model (Eq. 8): hidden state update driven by input increments using random Gaussian matrices A_i ~ xi_N scaled by 1/sqrt(N), with a nonlinearity phi applied to the state.

The plan outlines implementation of the RF-CDE discretization (Eq. 10): Euler update using lifted path increments Delta X_t^{F,i}, with random matrices sigma_A * A_i and bias sigma_b * b_i, and initial state sigma_0 * z_0.

The plan outlines implementation of the R-RDE log-ODE discretization (Eq. 16): state update using Pi_B applied to log-signature increments log_m(X_{t_i, t_{i+1}}), where B^(w) are nested Lie brackets of random matrices.

The plan includes the full preprocessing pipeline for UEA datasets: loading train/test splits, min-max scaling to [-1, 1], piecewise-linear interpolation, resampling sequences to length 200, and applying time and basepoint augmentation.

The plan specifies grid search hyperparameter ranges: sigma_A in {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 2.0}, sigma_B in {0.1, 0.25, 0.5}, sigma_0 in {0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5}, activation in {id, tanh, ReLU}, signature level M in {2,3,4,5}, and F in {32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024} for RF-CDE.

The Random Fourier Feature map phi_mu^F is implemented: frequencies omega_j are sampled i.i.d. from a Gaussian distribution, and the feature map produces (cos(omega_j^T x), sin(omega_j^T x)) concatenated and scaled by 1/sqrt(F), mapping R^d to R^{2F}.

The R-CDE forward pass (Eq. 8) is implemented: random Gaussian matrices A_i are drawn i.i.d. from xi_N, the hidden state evolves via Z_{t+1} = Z_t + (1/sqrt(N)) * sum_i A_i * phi(Z_t) * Delta x_t^i, with configurable nonlinearity phi.

The RF-CDE discretization (Eq. 10) is implemented: the driving signal x_t is first lifted through phi_mu^F to get X_t^F in R^{2F}, then the Euler update Delta Z_t = (1/sqrt(N)) * sum_i (sigma_A * A_i * phi(Z_t) + sigma_b * b_i) * Delta X_t^{F,i} is applied, with initial state Z_0 = sigma_0 * z_0.

The Gamma_A algebra homomorphism (Eq. 11) is implemented for R-RDE: A_w := (1/N^{k/2}) * A_{i1} * ... * A_{ik} for word w = i1...ik, and Gamma_A(G) = sum_{w in W_d} A_w * <G, w>, extended multiplicatively.

The log-ODE discretization for R-RDE (Eq. 16) is implemented: at each time step, the state is updated as Z_{t_{i+1}} = Z_{t_i} + Pi_B(X_{t_i, t_{i+1}}) * phi(Z_{t_i}), where Pi_B maps the log-signature increment via a sum over a Hall/Lyndon basis of Lie words, with B^(w) = (1/N^{k/2}) * nested Lie brackets of B_i matrices.

Log-signatures are computed and used as input to the R-RDE: given a rough path X, the log-signature log_m(X_{t_i, t_{i+1}}) of the path increment over each interval is computed using a truncated Hall/Lyndon basis at level m.

The linear readout is implemented as SVM or LinearSVM trained on the final hidden state Z_T^N extracted by the reservoir model, with no backpropagation through the random dynamics (training only the linear readout layer).

The preprocessing pipeline for UEA classification is implemented: datasets are loaded with their pre-specified train/test splits, min-max scaled to [-1, 1], sequences are resampled to length 200 via piecewise-linear interpolation, and time and basepoint augmentation are applied.
The optional lead-lag transform is implemented and tuned via grid search: for each input time series x_t, the lead-lag version interleaves x_t and x_{t-1} to create a 2d-dimensional path.

Feature normalization is implemented as an optional step tunable via grid search: after extracting reservoir features Z_T^N, the features are optionally normalized before passing to the SVM classifier.

The RFSF-DP baseline (Diagonal Projection variant of Random Fourier Signature Features, Toth et al. 2025) is implemented: random Fourier features are applied pointwise to the path, then the truncated signature is computed, with diagonal projection to control dimensionality.

The RFSF-TRP baseline (Tensorized Random Projection variant) is implemented: random Fourier features are applied pointwise to the path, then the truncated signature is computed with tensorized random projection.
The SigPDE baseline with RBF base kernel is implemented or interfaced: the signature kernel is computed as the solution to a Goursat PDE (Eq. 3), then used in an SVM.

The hyperparameter grid search is implemented: for each model, the specified hyperparameter grids are swept (sigma_A, sigma_b, sigma_0, activation, signature level M, feature count F, Fourier frequency scale), and the best configuration is selected on validation accuracy.

Fractional Brownian motion (fBm) data generation is implemented for the Hurst exponent classification task: using the Davies-Harte method, for each H in {0.05, 0.15, ..., 0.75} (8 classes), 50 training and 25 test samples are generated of length 256, stacking 3 independent realisations for d=3.

The V2 standardization variant for fBm classification is implemented: per-sample, per-dimension normalization (Eq. 27) subtracts the sample mean and divides by the sample standard deviation, forcing the model to exploit only geometric/temporal structure.

The missing data experiment is implemented: a binary mask M is generated by independently removing entries of the test path with probability p (p=20% and p=40%), missing values are imputed via linear interpolation along the time axis, and models are trained on clean data and evaluated on corrupted-interpolated data.

The JAX-based implementation (RandomSigJax) runs correctly: all random differential equation models (R-CDE, RF-CDE, R-RDE) are implemented in JAX and leverage GPU acceleration for efficient matrix operations and feature extraction.

The UEA time series classification experiment is executed for all 16 datasets with N=250 features, comparing R-CDE, RF-CDE, R-RDE, RFSF-DP, RFSF-TRP, and SigPDE, running each model 3 times and reporting median test accuracy.
The UEA classification ablation experiments are executed for N=64 and N=500 features, repeating the full protocol for all random-feature models and reporting mean test accuracy per dataset.

The Hurst exponent classification experiment is executed for both V1 (raw fBm) and V2 (standardized) settings, with N=64 and N=100 features, comparing R-CDE, RF-CDE, R-RDE, RFSF-DP, RFSF-TRP, NCDE, and NRDE.
The missing data robustness experiment is executed on UEA datasets with N=64 features under 20% and 40% corruption rates, reporting per-dataset accuracy and average accuracy % decrease.

The UEA classification results (Table 2, N=250) match the paper: RF-CDE achieves average accuracy of 0.741 and average rank of 3.062, R-RDE achieves 0.708, RFSF-DP 0.726, RFSF-TRP 0.725, SigPDE 0.738, and R-CDE 0.695.

The per-dataset UEA classification results (Table 2) are approximately reproduced: RF-CDE performs particularly well on tasks such as Libras (0.911), NATOPS (0.944), and StandWalkJump (0.667), while R-RDE leads on UWaveGestureLibrary (0.903).

The Hurst exponent classification results (Table 3) are approximately reproduced: R-RDE achieves the best accuracy in all four settings (V1-N=64: 0.955, V1-N=100: 0.950, V2-N=64: 0.735, V2-N=100: 0.730), and R-RDE outperforms RFSF and neural baselines in the challenging V2 regime.

The N=64 UEA ablation results (Table 4) are approximately reproduced: RF-CDE achieves the best average accuracy (0.713) and best average rank (2.50) at low feature budget, outperforming RFSF-DP (0.687) and RFSF-TRP (0.701) by a larger margin than at N=250.

The N=500 UEA ablation results (Table 5) are approximately reproduced: RFSF-DP and RFSF-TRP benefit more from increased features (0.747 and 0.737 respectively), while RF-CDE shows diminishing returns (0.733), and R-RDE shows a slight lift (0.711).

The missing data robustness results (Tables 7 and 8) are approximately reproduced: RF-CDE shows the most stable performance under corruption (average accuracy decrease of ~2.7% at 20% missing and ~5.7% at 40%), with better average rank than RFSF and R-RDE in both regimes.

The computational complexity results (Table 1) are consistent with the implementation: RF-CDE has O(B*l*F*(N^2+d)) feature extraction cost, R-CDE has O(B*l*N^2*d), and R-RDE has O(N^2*d^M*(B*l+N)), where the N^3 component in R-RDE is independent of batch size.
